transcript.text: Hi, Gaby.
Hi, Matthew.
How are you?
Just fine.
And yourself?
Yeah, great.
Thank you.
Wonderful.
Wonderful.
Good to hear.
Let's see.
Let's see.
Hello.
Hi.
Sorry, I left the calls today.
My name is Matthew.
I'm the chief content officer here at GrowthX.
Just a quick snapshot.
I am generally in charge of the kind of quality aspects of our content.
Chief content officer at a lot of other places might really refer to something more like marketing or marketing efforts externally.
For us, that's internal facing because of the client work that we're doing.
We need somebody to sort of be the ombudsman for or ombuds person for content quality and deliverables to make sure that the clients are getting good quality out of it.
And then working backwards from there to understand how we can improve that content by architecture.
For us, that generally means improving our pipelines and workflows that we use to generate the content or the process by which that content is being reviewed by content managers or managing editors.
Previous to GrowthX, I did a little bit of marketing and startups as a VP of marketing for a consumer startup.
And then I was the editor-in-chief of TechCrunch for about 10 years, ran our global news operations and all of our global events.
So, decent amount of experience in the content world.
And then got to talking with Marcel and Daniel about how they were thinking about the composability of content.
And it was kind of very similar to a lot of the things that I had been exploring.
I got excited about it and joined up.
So, that's why I am here.
Happy to hear a little bit about yourself.
Yeah, sure thing.
So, I started my career about 10 years ago at Accenture in London.
So, working as a management consulting analyst on Fortune 500 clients.
I'm to I'm to I'm to to to I'm I'm I'm I'm
And that's where I'd say, like, my kind of systems thinking started.
So just being immersed in that world of management consulting, looking at how we deploy these huge scale projects across massive clients.
But I've always been very entrepreneurial.
So I actually left Accenture around two or three years in to join a marketing agency where we focused on programmatic advertising.
It was called AppThis, operating out of Brighton, also in the UK.
And then kind of combining what I learned from Accenture and from AppThis, I decided to start my own thing where I kind of had my own systems, my own leadership around content.

So just started freelancing at first, doing copywriting for clients, and was quickly pulled as close to in-house as I was willing to go for two different international marketing agencies, where one of them, I am currently the head of content, and the other one, I'm working with their sort of strategic head of content.
On strategy and also a really kind of Gen Z tone of voice that we're trying to develop for our clients at the moment.
So that's something that I'm just generally interested in is like the cutting edge of content, how tone and voice is changing based on new audiences.
So it's kind of meeting my two passions there.
And that kind of picks us up to today.
I also on the weekends, I'm completing a master's degree in creative writing.
So content really is my whole thing, my whole life at the moment.
Just because I've always had a massive love of storytelling, just huge nerds in general, love sci-fi, love fiction.
So that's kind of meeting that need on the side.
But it does really feed my content work with my clients as well.
Just bringing that human edge to all the AI tools we're using and all the AI workflows that I'm developing, making sure that like storytelling, the oldest human art is still at center.
So yeah, that's great.
Thank you.
I appreciate it.
Yeah.
It's a, it's certainly an interesting world.
mean, using AI tools or LLMs to generate content is very much like trying to, you know, park a Lamborghini parallel, right?

It's a very powerful tool, but very easy to hit the gas and ram into the Honda Accord in front of you, not to stretch the analogy too far, but it's very, very easy to get bad results, but authoritatively bad.
You're like, oh, this has like, at first glance, the feel of quality content.
then you actually get it and you're like, oh, no, it's not.
It's actually very, very good.
Which is the world we live in.
So that's, that's good to hear.
Talk to a little bit about like, you know, kind of like the AI tooling and workflows that you use either personally or for clients.
I would love to hear a little bit about that.

Just kind of get as granular as you feel like, because we're trying to get an idea for folks coming into this position, how much experience they have in that, because they're part of the role of course, is hip pocket understanding of content and what.

The mouthfeel of good content is like, but beyond that, how to articulate the process of getting there well enough to improve the pipelines for any individual client, and then, of course, improve the system overall for all clients.
And that process is not necessarily natural to many people coming from the content world.
And so we have been very explicitly looking for people who have done work with content, but also with workflows and AI tooling.
So I'd love to hear about your experiences using those.
Yeah, absolutely.

So I'll say that when I was first looking into GrowthX, I was excited because it feels like what you're doing is the huge scale version of the processes that I've been implementing as head of content in KCG, which is my current one of the international marketing agencies I work for.
And I've been implementing that on a much smaller scale or a much smaller agency, but within my content team, I've been using...

I'm money.com, obviously, for project management, Jasper AI for tone of voice development, everything from kind of then shipping off like abandoned cart email campaigns through to blog post generation, through to everything to do with like keywords on landing pages.
So Jasper's been really good for that.
I use Notion AI not as part of KCG.
That's just with some of my other clients I take on because I actually just like it as a program and a product.
And then I also use ChatGPT and Gemini for content production.
And the way I use it is I was working up until a few months ago.
I had two or three writers under me at Kingscrest.
So I was using it as essentially project management and systems development tools.
So starting off with monday.com, that's how I would assign out projects for my writers.
So we have a bunch of different clients at KCG, ranging from software as a service through to like consumer facing brands.
And for them, we need everything from blogs to app copy to throughout the whole content ecosystem and life cycle.
Obviously, we need content.
So it's about building that in monday.com and then assigning out with deadlines to my writers.
And then I've trained them on using ChatGPT with sort of established tone of voice guidelines per client so that we're all operating from the same playbook.
That's really important.
And then from there, basically just project management and tracking, weekly client meetings.

I work with the project managers in our team closely, but as I'm the head of content, I'm sort of the client-facing person for our clients who goes ahead and speaks around what they're looking for, what their goals are, and how we're tracking against the project deadlines.
So those are kind of tools I use day to day.
I also do really pride myself on my ability to learn new tools.
It's something, a curiosity that kind of followed me since my Accenture days.
I feel confident.

Getting to grips with new tech, and there's so much out there right now, but I'm a very curious person, and competitive person, so I'm always looking to, yeah, sort of be better than the next person at figuring that out.
Yeah, curiosity and competitiveness are great in startups.
You know, it's an interesting world, because there's a general elasticity to what we do.
Um, there, we're always outstripping our current capabilities, and then catching up to them, and then outstripping them, and then catching up.
Um, that is the startup way.
If you are not doing that, you are generally moving too slow.
Um, there is a balance to be struck there, for sure, and sometimes the balance can be painful to re-attain.
Um, and, and that's why we have humans, right?
It's not all just robots all the way down.
Uh, the humans can catch the things, and understand them, and then help push it forward.
Um, I will say, like, for people that are systems-oriented, and it seems like you...
You know, have built your own systems, which is wonderful, because many people have not or only have utilized other systems.
It's a fun place because there is a feeling of ability to impact process that is absent at a lot of places where you're using like an internal tool that is built off in some other world somewhere.
And you're like, hey, I can file a ticket, but who knows if that'll ever get addressed, or maybe it's a cycle six months from now or, you know, all of that.
Whereas we are very rapidly developing our internal product, which I think you said one of the companies you worked for was called Atlas.
Is that correct?
No, sorry.
That might be Accenture.
No, you mentioned either a client or somebody called Atlas or Atlas.
No, no.
Okay.
Okay.
My mistake.
So anyhow, our internal tool is called Atlas.
Atlas.
That's why I heard that you said that, and I was like, oh, what is this?
Any...
Yeah, long story short, our internal tool is called Atlas.
We built it because we were using a handful of external workflow tools, not too dissimilar from on Monday.com, but we were using, I think, primarily Aerofs previously.
Aerofs is a workflow tool that allows you to connect various workflows together, similar to building automation in Zapier, but just a little bit deeper.
However, the limitations that we ran up against with Aerofs were largely to do with adaptability and speed.
Like, slow, we couldn't do a lot of things we wanted to do and build around it and on top of it.
So nothing against it, we just outgrew it.
And we started building our own tool.
The tool that we have now does a handful of things really well, and then a lot of things not so well.
And we're working to make all the things work well, right?
That's the state that we're in.
But it is, at its core, a workflows tool.
So you're able to...
To build out workflows for a client and then attach those workflows together into a pipeline that hopefully delivers work product, right?
So as an example, to take one of the most simple work streams that we have, it would be the inputs being like a topic.

Maybe it's a keyword-driven topic, generating a brief outline, doing research on that, and then a draft, and then all of the follow-up stuff, adapting that draft to appropriate voice and tone, fact-checking it.
So, you know, LLM is just to hallucinate stuff, obviously, nonstop.
So we built our own fact-checker, which is pretty good.
It's one of the things that actually works very well out of all of the pipeline stuff.
Then internal linking, external linking, metadata and tagging, image generation, if necessary, and then even staging and publishing.
And it can all happen within one pipeline.
So the CS...
The or content manager's job is to manage.
The reason the manager is in that title, they're not managing people, they're managing a pipeline or a series of pipelines for clients.
And then the managing editor is managing the CMs.
And so part of the unlock that a managing editor can provide is identifying the process issues that a CM is encountering and then articulating fixes or engaging the right resources to fix those.
And sometimes those can be pure process, like they're just doing it wrong.
You you're holding it wrong type scenario.
Then some of those can be pipeline or workflow based.
Hey, I see you're having a problem and I'm pretty sure it's the pipeline that's causing this problem.
Either it's delivering poor quality content or it's not doing, you know, the right thing or whatever.
And then sometimes it's inputs and outputs.
It's just, you are not engineering the context properly, context being the artifacts that we feed the pipeline, whether that's like the company overview.
You.
You.
A detail of its products, its personas, like the audience personas that it is speaking to, or it's, of course, writing guidelines, what most people would call voice and tone, right?
But we can get hyper-specific with those.
So it's like, hey, you want, you know, periods in certain places?
Fine.
I don't like it, but you do, so we'll make sure it happens.
Or it defies all of the logic of, you know, the English language, but this is what you want?
Fine.
We can adhere to that.
And like, that's, those are rules that we can write for the pipelines.
But any one of those may end up being a process blocker for a content manager.
And generally, the way that manifests is speed, right?
They're unable to get their work out on time, or they're unable to meet deadlines, or they feel like they have to bare-knuckle everything to get it there, which leads to burnout, right?
Because long-term, they're going to have a hard time.
Because we are offering content at scale to our clients, and that scale is beyond, frankly, what a human should be able to
Do.
Because otherwise, why are we a company, right?
So I think that there's that aspect of things that I think is important to understand coming in.
And I'd also love to hear about it.
You mentioned you have worked with writers.
So talk to me through how you work with them to improve process, meet deadlines, that sort of thing.
I'd love to hear about any personal experience there.
Yeah, for sure.

So something that I sort of came up against pretty early on is something that I think I touched on when I was previously kind of introducing that concept, which is that we did have and we do have so many different clients across industries and with various different audience bases, with various different goals, products, industries that they're coming from.
And so trying to obviously assign writers to each of those project pipelines was something that was very manual.
So I knew my writers, I knew their background, I knew what they were good at.
So I'd assign them.
But from there, I mean...
when we started this like seven years ago, they were manually writing everything themselves, pulling keywords themselves based on their own, going off and researching that by themselves.
And it was just taking such a long time.
And also, you know, it's an agency environment.
So you're also getting in quick requests from high value clients and you're having to basically adapt that in real time.

And I was finding that actually what was happening was I was taking on the load of all of the last minute requests we were getting while my team focused on the, following the pipeline that I had set out for them.
And they were doing so slowly because it was a slow and manual process.
So then as we started to look into more and more tools, I was able to create repositories of working with the SEO teams to get the keywords already in place.
They're not doing that themselves anymore.
We're working with the tools and the experts to get that shipped off to them so that they've got it as part of their original brief.
Then we also have, you know, where are you pulling links from?
What kind of links are putting into blogs, for example, for example, to make sure that these are sort of high.

Fidelity and trustworthy articles, just basically building this content from the ground up and putting those building blocks together in a way that they would have then a brief to take and then get writing.
And as AI tools started to come in, we were then able to actually outsource a lot of that writing to the tools as well, saving even more time.
So that was really good in itself.

Now, when it came to the last minute requests and me taking on a lot of that, then I just started to look at how my project management was running, look at prioritizing based on client value, client size, where we could also sub out work that was, for example, okay, so we're not going to meet, sometimes you're just not going to meet a deadline, right?
We're not going to meet that deadline.

What can we do that still provides value to the client and that also fits in with the system and pipeline that I've created so my writers can work on it and I can focus on client management and relationship management and still provide them with an MVP, they'll be happy.
So it's kind of blending that human systems.
And in particular, we had one very difficult, and this is so random.
He was a hair transplant doctor in New York, and he was just an incredibly difficult man.
And he was a small account, and he had constant revisions for us, constant sort of shifting baselines, shifting goalposts.
So being able to also then work with my pipeline and see, okay, actually, we need to have a much clearer definition of what we're doing with him.
That he understands, and that we understand, so that we can achieve goals and create a situation that he's happy with.

So basically, just communicating that, working it back into the pipeline, the fixes we've created with him, and then essentially making a custom pipeline for him, whilst also putting in boundaries with him, that was also key.
You're not going to get five revisions by Tuesday, because that's not possible.
What we will provide is two revisions by the end of the week.
And so just working across that whole pipeline, from my writers through to the client.
And I was...
I see it less of a pipeline, more of an ecosystem, because they all feed into each other.
But that was kind of a key example I can think of where we were then able to really start hitting those deadlines.
He was happy, everything was running smoothly, and it just got better over the years as AI tools came in and we were able to automate.
Yeah, no, that's great.
That makes sense.
And that kind of work and kind of mode of thinking does fit in with a lot of the stuff that we do and the stuff that the MEs are doing.

You know, it's a little bit of client management, it's a little bit of understanding work product, and then also, of process and pipeline improvement, you know, whether that's the proverbial pipeline or our actual pipelines.
I think it's a, you know, it's a balancing act.
Obviously, clients are important, but not infallible, and, you know, are often unreasonable.
And so being able to push back and say, like, hey, you know, can we find another way to achieve this thing that you want to achieve in an actual achievable way?
Or frankly, a lot of.
Unfortunately, the more stakeholders you have on a call, the more chances you have of having somebody who thinks they're an expert.
And generally speaking, they're not, right?
I mean, they can be.

They can be, you know, you can get diamonds in the rough every now and then you get somebody who really understands the timescale in which organic content works and then like the feel of good content and all that.
Yeah, all too often, unfortunately, what you do get is English majors and you're like, hey, this is marketing content that's, you know, for a specific purpose.
It's not an English essay, right?
And it is not, it's not Billy Shakespeare, right?
Like there are certain aspects of this that need to be the way that they are, architecturally speaking.
At the same time, they also need to be read for humans.
And like, that's one of the strengths of our system is that we're able to balance those goals and mostly achieve all of them.
So they can be SEO ready.
They can be geo ready as far as the best.
I guess you'd call it industry best practices, although there are none, right?
Like even the LLMs themselves, the teams working there have no idea, really.

So we're just working off of reading the tea leaves on Geo and implementing best practices there, whether that's something like a structural issue in an article or something on their site, like at an llms.txt that we want to implement properly.

You know, so they trust us, hopefully, to do those things well, but the balancing of all of that with the thing that I, you know, always try to remind everybody that it also needs to be readable by a human.
Like that aspect of balancing, which I'm sure you have done over the years working with clients a lot, is something where we are the experts and they are not.
And like managing client expectation and expecting them to understand and rock all of that stuff is usually a fool's errand.
So being able to manage that externally, but also be honest internally about, hey, we failed.
And we can do better, right?
We can actually do this that they want us to do.
That's what it's all about.
And, you know, I'm glad you've had some experience there.
I think that's definitely valuable in this environment.
We're currently working with like nearly 70 clients and we'll probably be working with more in the future.
We want to scale quite a bit more.
What that generally means is there's a good amount of them that have an overlap where you can define a process.
And architect and sort of generate like you are the genesis of a new process, whether that's a new work stream or a new way of doing things.
And that absolutely almost always will have some effects on the product that is additive to other people's work.
So there are things that you can do where you're like, hey, you know, this client really needs this.
We built this.
We filed a ticket.
We got it working.
It's polished.
It works well.
And other people at the company are going to be like, I'm so glad you did this because we had the same problem.
And this did not exist, and now it does.
And that is fun.
That's cool work.
You can actually directly affect the product.
You can affect its outcome and its development process.
And it can materially benefit you and your team and your clients on a very short timescale.
Like, hey, we built a new feature, tested it, shifted within a couple of weeks, and now you're using this to deliver client work.
But it can, of course, have added effects on the whole product long term.
There's plenty of features that are in Atlas now that were basically born of client need, articulated by the MEs, and then implemented by the engineering team.
And that's part of the process.
If you like that, if it's interesting to you, work here.
If you're like, that sounds like my nightmare, I like to work with steady state processes and increase their efficiencies, don't come work here.
Because it is a lot about that.
That elasticity is anathema to some people, and it is catnip to others.
And like that...
That ability to stay lit in your feet, all of that stuff, super germane to the job.
And I just like to be honest about it, right, up front.
The other aspects that normally come along with that, like unreasonable expectations, toxicity, opacity, those don't really exist here.
Like people are generally pretty read in on what we're doing.
There's a transparency to where the business is headed, what we're developing.
You can read the EPD channel and watch all the tickets getting filed and worked on if you so choose.
I do every day, but not everybody does, but I do.
And, know, because I love to see what's happening and where we're headed.
So there's tons of that, plenty of documentation, very generally good people work here all the way up to the leadership.
So no real issues as far as the work environment goes.
And then we all have realistic expectations.
Look, look, we need to deliver the client work, but we understand this.
Some of it is bare knuckle.
Some of it is processed well.
And some of it is in between.
And we obviously need to work on getting it more towards the process driven.
And that work is the job, right?
Like the job is yes, getting client work out, but it is absolutely also process improvement.
So that's the kind of focus we're looking for.
Does that make sense?
Yeah, yeah, definitely.
And that really speaks to me in terms of having that sort of hands on the wheel approach to it.
I think that the best way to iterate and improve is through lived experience of the people who are using that tool every day.
So it really speaks to me.
Yeah.
Hey, look, our focus group is our MEs and CMs, right?
Like, instead of shipping the product publicly and going, I hope somebody uses this and it's good, right?
We have this focus group of people using it day in, day out for the purpose it was intended for.
And frankly, for some purposes, it was not intended for.
And we're like,
Should we build that?
We can build that.
And I think that's the fun bit.
So do you have any questions for me?
Yeah, a few actually.
So first kind of question is a bit high level, but looking into growthx, having seen a lot of that early investment, it's obviously super exciting.
And so it's just super interested in hearing kind of where you see a company going in the next year, two years, what the goals are long term.
Yeah, if you give me some insight into that.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I mean, look, there's a couple of near term or midterm goals, which are be the best possible platform internally first, and then externally.

We will expose it to external users at some point, but really be the best platform for end to end content generation, understanding the performance of content and being able to articulate the processes well enough to where anybody can.
Use it, even somebody with limited content experience.
So right now we have people with a lot of content experience using it.
And over time, we need to architect it in a way where people with more limited content experience will still get real value out of it.

And that's going to be difficult, you know, because I'm sure you've seen it firsthand where people who don't have a lot of content experience are essentially at sea when it comes to evaluating whether content is actually good or, more importantly, whether it will achieve long-term goals, whether that's traffic or conversions or authority building or link building, whatever goals they may have.
They're unable to really articulate, oh, well, it needs to have these elements and these are the ones that perform well.
We are doing really interesting and fairly unique work in that we are collecting data about what works, what functions down to like the word choice level and what delivers on goals.

Most of the time, when a company interacts with an agency or a consultant, which you have been this so you understand, they are working with you and they're getting the advantage of your expertise and working with you and whatever lift you bring to their business.
The moment they stop working with you, they stop having that advantage.
They have whatever past learned experience they have from you.
Maybe they have some docs that you shared with them, et cetera, where you're like, hey, here's some offboarding docs.
Here's the process that I used.
Okay, have fun.
You know, that's great.
But there is a world where that process gets collated, understood, measured, and architected into pipelines.
And that's the work that we're doing.
So understanding that helps you to kind of rock where, like if you see an engineering, you know, motion being put in place, you can kind of see where it's headed towards that goaling.
Because right now, all of this stuff is like, choose your own adventure.
So
Fathom tools that offer no real immediate value.
It's like, cool, learn the content business, and then this tool will make your job slightly easier.
It's like, no, you know, like that's, don't do that, right?
Like there's plenty of people that do that and make a lot of money at it.
Congratulations to them.
But their churn rates are insane because people get in there and they're like, I don't know, you know, they poke at a few buttons and then eventually get, what do they do?
They hire a consultant to run the thing, right?
Or they hire several people to run the thing, you know, internally, and they spend a lot of money on top of spending a lot of money.
So what we are trying to do is do enough work with clients to understand that world well enough and architect those things.
And then even build the architecture for them to build solutions without all of the experience in pipeline architecting.
So this is not built, so asterisk, but the ability for somebody to generate a pipeline on the fly using...

Natural language to say, hey, okay, I really want to write articles about this topic, and like, here's my company, and I'd really love it to be this, and wave their hands a lot, and then for the pipeline to be generated out of that, that can then reliably and repeatedly generate that quality content.
Like, that is a goal, right?
Then overarching, long-term goals, besides the, hey, we're going to expose this to users, and now they'll be able to use the platform and get all the benefits of all the learning that we did.
Content generation and content operations is just one business process, and we are actually building the architecture to understand business processes.
That's what we're doing.
We're not just building a good content generator.
We are building a process that understands work processes and architects them into pipelines.
So, one easy way to think about this is, what if this was a sales motion instead of a content?
Could you architect your business processes for sales into a repeatable process?
Yes, you can, but normally what that does, the way that happens is it happens in the brains of the salespeople that you hire.
Your top performing salespeople are masters of their craft.

They're really good at understanding things like customer signaling, follow-up routines, all of that stuff, like manipulating the various tools at their disposal, whether it's, you know, Gong or HubSpot or whatever, like they just have all of that in their head.
When they leave, hey, it's great.
I'm so glad you were with us.
Awesome, cool new opportunity you have.
Now they're gone.
Where do all those processes go?
They leave with them, right?
And even more so if you train them up from nothing.
Let's say you bring in somebody junior and you've trained them in all of your process and all this stuff.
And where do those processes live?
In other people, typically.
Those other people train them.
They make them better at their job.
They...
Articulate this, okay, so when you do this, here's the if, then, then from that point, like, okay, if they say this, here's what you say, right?
Or if they do that, then do this, right?
Those processes generally vanish with the people and like, there's no reason they need to.
You can't architect processes in a flow that offers enough variability to adapt without you having to find thousands of individual rules.
That's what LLMs are good at.
So if you provide them the proper validations and guardrails and context, they are actually quite good at articulating responses to processes and that sort of thing.
So the long-term goal is what if we could architect other business processes in a similar way to content?
Wow, that's super inspirational.
Wow.
Yeah, that sounds awesome.
mean, just creating that architecture to take it from that roadblock of just everything being in one person.
Fathom's head, taking it and turning it into something that's a tool for everybody.
Yeah, that's really cool.
Wow.
Yeah.
Awesome.
So, I mean, you've worked with great writers.
You've seen it happen live where you give them a brief and then they turn you out some finished copy and you're like, wow, like this is really great.
This is really like what a great logical framing here or this is an amazing narrative flourish or or, you know, how did you come up with this?
How did you do this?
they're like, I don't know.
Yeah, it went in here and it came out here.
Right.
And I've worked with a lot of writers, a lot of writers, thousands.
And I can tell you the number of them that can actually articulate the way that they build those things is vanishingly small.
Generally speaking, they get retired and they turn into editors because that's what editors are generally good at.
But it's rare to have somebody who's good at writing in a good enough way to like actually graduate to be an editor, but can still write.
Right.
And that.

what our pipelines do, you know, like, and that articulation process of understanding what they, the way the pipeline is operating and where the inputs or outputs may have gone awry or where the context needs to be re-engineered, that's the kind of people we're looking for.
When we say process architects, when it comes to content, that's really what we need.
Like, you know, hey, how do you get to here from there?
Like, what are the architectural landmarks that need to be established in order to achieve solid output and repeatedly solid output?
Yeah, it almost feels like a neural model of kind of the best of not just one brain, but hundreds of thousands of brains.
And, you know, we were talking about best practice before and how there is no best practice.
It's almost kind of creating this multitude of best practices based on performance over time.
It's interesting.
really, yeah, that is speaking to me.
